invalid}}''' }}
'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.
== Lineage ==
* Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} (1655–1717) * Mother: Unknown * Foster Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Wife: Unknown * Daughter: Married to {{ill|Takatsuji Fukunaga|ja|高辻福長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuji Fukunaga|1=Takatsuji Tominaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672893}} * Adopted Son: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|ja|随筆百花苑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|1=Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658952}} * {{ill|Halley's Comet|ja|ハレー彗星#紀元前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Halley's Comet|1=Halley's Comet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23054}}

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.

== Career ==

Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>

In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.

During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).

Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.

== Legacy ==

Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>

He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>

== Works ==

In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

invalid}}''' }}
'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.
== Lineage ==
* Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} (1655–1717) * Mother: Unknown * Foster Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Wife: Unknown * Daughter: Married to {{ill|Takatsuji Fukunaga|ja|高辻福長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuji Fukunaga|1=Takatsuji Tominaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672893}} * Adopted Son: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|ja|随筆百花苑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|1=Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658952}} * {{ill|Halley's Comet|ja|ハレー彗星#紀元前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Halley's Comet|1=Halley's Comet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23054}}

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>

Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>

invalid}}''' }}
'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.
== Lineage ==
* Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} (1655–1717) * Mother: Unknown * Foster Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Wife: Unknown * Daughter: Married to {{ill|Takatsuji Fukunaga|ja|高辻福長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuji Fukunaga|1=Takatsuji Tominaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672893}} * Adopted Son: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|ja|随筆百花苑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|1=Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658952}} * {{ill|Halley's Comet|ja|ハレー彗星#紀元前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Halley's Comet|1=Halley's Comet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23054}}

Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

invalid}}''' }}
'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] nobleman and [[:en:School of Naturalists|Onmyōji]]. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In [[:en:Kan'en|Kanen]] 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]]. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th [[:en:Shogun|shōgun]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]], implemented in [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|solar eclipses]] emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa]] 8 (1771). Yasukuni's [[:en:Hōryaku calendar|Hōryaku calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Kansei calendar|Kansei calendar]] in [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>

In [[:en:Hōreki|Hōryaku]] 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} nobleman and {{ill|School of Naturalists|en|School of Naturalists|WD=Q1195540|lt=Onmyōji}}. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|WD=Q1145723|lt=Kanen}} 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court}}. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=shōgun}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the {{ill|Hōryaku calendar|en|Hōryaku calendar|WD=Q2352988|lt=Hōryaku calendar}}, implemented in {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōryaku}} 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting {{ill|Solar eclipse|en|Solar eclipse|WD=Q3887|lt=solar eclipses}} emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa}} 8 (1771). Yasukuni's {{ill|Hōryaku calendar|en|Hōryaku calendar|WD=Q2352988|lt=Hōryaku calendar}} was replaced by the {{ill|Kansei calendar|en|Kansei calendar|WD=Q2355562|lt=Kansei calendar}} in {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|WD=Q1195131|lt=Kansei}} 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōryaku}} 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.
== Lineage ==
* Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} (1655–1717) * Mother: Unknown * Foster Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Wife: Unknown * Daughter: Married to {{ill|Takatsuji Fukunaga|ja|高辻福長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuji Fukunaga|1=Takatsuji Tominaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672893}} * Adopted Son: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|ja|随筆百花苑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|1=Zuihitsu Hyakkaen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658952}} * {{ill|Halley's Comet|ja|ハレー彗星#紀元前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Halley's Comet|1=Halley's Comet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23054}}

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} nobleman and {{ill|School of Naturalists|en|School of Naturalists|WD=Q1195540|lt=Onmyōji}}. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.
== Career ==
Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>
In {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|WD=Q1145723|lt=Kanen}} 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court}}. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=shōgun}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the {{ill|Hōryaku calendar|en|Hōryaku calendar|WD=Q2352988|lt=Hōryaku calendar}}, implemented in {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōryaku}} 5 (1755).
Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting {{ill|Solar eclipse|en|Solar eclipse|WD=Q3887|lt=solar eclipses}} emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa}} 8 (1771). Yasukuni's {{ill|Hōryaku calendar|en|Hōryaku calendar|WD=Q2352988|lt=Hōryaku calendar}} was replaced by the {{ill|Kansei calendar|en|Kansei calendar|WD=Q2355562|lt=Kansei calendar}} in {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|WD=Q1195131|lt=Kansei}} 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.
== Legacy ==
Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>
He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>
== Works ==
In {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōryaku}} 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.
== Lineage ==
* Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} (1655–1717) * Mother: Unknown * Foster Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Wife: Unknown * Daughter: Married to {{ill|Takatsuji Fukunaga|ja|高辻福長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuji Fukunaga|1=Takatsuji Tominaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672893}} * Adopted Son: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

'''Tsuchimikado Yasukuni''' (土御門 泰邦) was a mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} nobleman and {{ill|School of Naturalists|en|School of Naturalists|WD=Q1195540|lt=Onmyōji}}. He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He held the court Rank of {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and served as {{ill|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja|治部卿|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}.

== Career ==

Yasukuni's elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja|泰誠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいせい|1=Taisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514067}}, the heir of their father Yasufuku, predeceased their father. Consequently, Yasuren inherited the family estate but remained childless. Yasukuni was adopted by Yasuren, inheriting the family on the condition that Yasuren’s biological son, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, would later succeed him.<ref>Yakei and his son {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja|土御門有邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yūhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} predeceased Yasukuni. {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja|土御門泰信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yashinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, adopted from the {{ill|Hagiwara family|ja|萩原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagiwara family|1=Hagiwara family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620328}}, was later dismissed, and {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja|土御門泰栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a descendant of the Kurahashi branch and Yasukuni’s great-grandson, became his son-in-law and successor.</ref>

In {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|WD=Q1145723|lt=Kanen}} 3 (1750), Yasukuni assumed his elder brother's Rank of {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}}.

During the Edo period, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} monopolized the authority to revise the calendar, stripping it from the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court}}. Yasukuni supported independent calendrical scholars like {{ill|Nishimura Enri|ja|西村遠里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishimura Enri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to challenge this. The 8th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=shōgun}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, attempted to revise the calendar using Western astronomy but passed away, leaving the initiative incomplete. Yasukuni capitalized on this to lead the creation of the {{ill|Hōryaku calendar|en|Hōryaku calendar|WD=Q2352988|lt=Hōryaku calendar}}, implemented in {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōryaku}} 5 (1755).

Although this earned him the title {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} and {{ill|Minister of Civil Affairs|ja|治部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minister of Civil Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10829855}}, inaccuracies in predicting {{ill|Solar eclipse|en|Solar eclipse|WD=Q3887|lt=solar eclipses}} emerged soon after, prompting further revisions in {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa}} 8 (1771). Yasukuni's {{ill|Hōryaku calendar|en|Hōryaku calendar|WD=Q2352988|lt=Hōryaku calendar}} was replaced by the {{ill|Kansei calendar|en|Kansei calendar|WD=Q2355562|lt=Kansei calendar}} in {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|WD=Q1195131|lt=Kansei}} 9 (1797), only 13 years after his death.

== Legacy ==

Yasukuni authored {{ill|Rokoku I|ja|漏刻緯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokoku I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a study of {{ill|clepsydras|ja|漏刻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clepsydras|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水時計|1=water clock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q194173}}, and contributed to the {{ill|Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja|宝暦暦法新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryaku Calendar Compendium|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, though the clepsydras he attempted to reconstruct were flawed.<ref>Kinoshita Masashi, "Research and Clepsydras in the Edo Period," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2020, ISBN 978-4-642-04657-2, pp. 57, 59–63, 71.</ref>

He was interred at {{ill|Bairinji|ja|梅林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bairinji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 梅林寺 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Bairin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q803940}} in Kyoto, alongside other {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} members, though no family contact remains.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Graves of Abe no Seimei’s Descendants at Risk|url=https://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/local/article/20170211000098|newspaper=Kyoto Shimbun|date=February 11, 2017}}</ref>

== Works ==

In {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōryaku}} 10 (1760), Yasukuni recorded his journey to Edo in {{ill|Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja|東行話説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgyō Wasetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which offers insights into the culinary culture of the Tōkaidō.

== Lineage ==

* Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} (1655–1717)
* Mother: Unknown
* Foster Father: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* Wife: Unknown
* Daughter: Married to {{ill|Takatsuji Fukunaga|ja|高辻福長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsuji Fukunaga|1=Takatsuji Tominaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672893}}
* Adopted Son: {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja|泰兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yakei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

He was the youngest son of {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasufuku|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} and later adopted by his elder brother {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja|土御門泰連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
